gm.name: Eliot Wolf
gm.title: Executive Vice President of Player Personnel
gm.previous_role: Director of Scouting, New England Patriots (promoted internally)

career_path.role: Draft Room Assistant / Son of GM Ron Wolf
Executive Vice President of Player Personnel (de facto GM)

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Ron Wolf (father, Hall of Fame GM) and Ted Thompson (Packers GM 2005-2017)
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Ron Wolf tree → Ted Thompson branch → Belichick cross-pollination. Ron Wolf's scouting DNA (simple grading scale, trust your scouts, BPA-oriented, emphasis on character and toughness) passed directly to Eliot. Ted Thompson refined this with extreme patience and draft-centric roster building. Belichick years added emphasis on scheme versatility and positional value flexibility. The Packers tree also produced John Schneider (Seahawks), John Dorsey (Chiefs/Browns), Scot McCloughan, and Reggie McKenzie — all share core evaluation principles around work ethic and thorough scouting.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Wolf willing to be more aggressive in need-filling than Thompson was (Thompson famously drafted Aaron Rodgers over need positions). Wolf's 2024 draft was heavily offense-oriented (7 of 8 picks), a departure from classic Packers balance. Also showed willingness to take OT with shorter arms (Campbell at 32 5/8 inches) that Thompson might have avoided — prioritizing film over measurable thresholds at certain positions.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: 2 full drafts as decision-maker provide growing data. Lineage weight dropping from initial 0.75. Ron Wolf's simple grading philosophy and character emphasis remain clearly visible. Belichick influence visible in trade-back tendency and special-teams picks on Day 3.

front_office.primary_decision_maker: Eliot Wolf (with Mike Vrabel having significant input and final say on personnel in 2025)

key_influencers.name: Mike Vrabel
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Defensive-minded, values toughness and physicality. Came from Titans system. Emphasis on culture-fit players. Prefers physical, defensive-minded football. Dynasty-era Patriot player — internalized Belichick culture. Had final say on personnel decisions in 2025 season.

key_influencers.name: Ryan Cowden
key_influencers.title: Vice President of Player Personnel
key_influencers.known_biases: Carolina Panthers scouting tree (16 years). Systematic thinker. Emphasizes character evaluation heavily — stated 'if you don't get the person right, then a lot of that other stuff can go by the wayside.' Values communication skills and football IQ in prospects. Worked with Vrabel in Tennessee for 5 years. Interim Titans GM in 2022.

key_influencers.name: Ron Wolf
key_influencers.title: Father / Informal Advisor (retired Hall of Fame GM)
key_influencers.known_biases: BPA advocate. Eliot speaks with his father daily and sends him daily transaction reports. Ron publicly advocated for taking best player available when Eliot had pick 3 in 2024. Still watches film and offers opinions.

front_office.power_dynamics: Collaborative triumvirate — Wolf, Vrabel, and Cowden work together. Vrabel had final say on personnel in 2025 but the relationship has become genuinely collaborative. Albert Breer reported 'the relationship between Mike Vrabel and Eliot Wolf worked from the start.' Wolf is formally the top personnel executive. Vrabel's coaching staff influences which archetypes fit the scheme.
front_office.owner_interference: low — Robert Kraft observed the Wolf-Mayo working relationship before formalizing Wolf's role in 2024. Kraft and son Jonathan ran HC search directly but do not appear to meddle in draft decisions. Kraft publicly praised Wolf's management style.

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Take the best player available. Wolf stated at 2026 combine: 'If you're doing your job, you're going to be able to find players rounds one through seven that can help your team.' Ron Wolf advocated for BPA when advising his son.
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Leans need in practice. In 2024, 7 of 8 picks were offensive players after a historically bad offensive season. Round 1 picks have addressed biggest needs both years (QB in 2024, OT in 2025). But willing to take BPA when need and value align — Campbell at 4 was BPA and need simultaneously. Cowden stated philosophy of 'investing in the people' and prioritizing cultural fit alongside talent.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Claims BPA but both first-round picks addressed the team's single biggest need. Need heavily influenced Day 2 picks in both drafts. 2024 was almost exclusively offense; 2025 Day 1-2 was all offense before pivoting to defense on Day 3.

trade_triggers: Accumulating additional Day 2 and Day 3 picks via trade-downs
Sliding back a few spots when their target will still be available
Acquiring future draft capital (took 2026 4th via trade-down in 2025 R3)
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Prefers accumulating picks by trading back. In 2024, traded down from 34 to 37 in R2 to gain an extra pick. In 2025, made two trade-downs from pick 77 (to 85, then to 95) to accumulate extra picks. Attempted to trade back into R1 in 2024 but couldn't find a deal. Has shown willingness to use R1 pick as trade bait — Wolf said at 2026 combine 'We're open to anything if we think it's going to help the team.' Belichick influence clearly visible in trade-back tendencies.

position_avoidance: LB in early rounds — no off-ball LBs drafted in first 4 rounds across 2 drafts despite defense needing help. TE has been Day 3 only.
position_premium: OL — 5 of 19 total picks across 2 drafts were offensive linemen including R1 pick in 2025. WR — drafted 3 WRs across 2 drafts in first 4 rounds. EDGE acknowledged as 'obvious need' heading into 2026, suggesting high priority.

QB.archetype_summary: Prototypical pocket passer with elite arm talent, dual-threat mobility, and poise under pressure. Wants a big, athletic QB who can make every throw and extend plays. Values arm strength and processing over pure running ability. Drake Maye embodies the archetype: big frame, cannon arm, mobile enough to scramble but wins from the pocket.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear threshold — Maye measured 32.25 inches
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm velocity is the top measurable. Mobility matters but is not about 40 time — it's about pocket movement and play extension. Joe Milton III (6-5, 246, elite arm) drafted late as a developmental prospect further emphasizes arm talent priority.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a primary factor for QB. Maye's 4.60 pro day time shows adequate mobility is sufficient.

playing_style.primary_style: Pocket passer with enough athleticism to extend plays and navigate pressure. Dual-threat capability preferred but not run-first.
least_important_traits: Designed rushing ability — will not draft a run-first QB as a franchise option
deal_breakers: Inadequate arm strength
Run-first playing style as primary value proposition
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Exceptional arm talent. Maye was taken at 3 as the clear choice; Milton drafted in R6 purely on arm strength as a development piece.

college_production.production_floor: Must have been a multi-year starter in a competitive conference. Maye: 2 years as starter, 7,929 passing yards, 62 TDs at UNC.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values consistency and ascending performance. Maye's 2022 breakout was elite; 2023 showed ability to maintain production with worse supporting cast.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High. Adjusted for Maye's poor OL and declining supporting cast at UNC. Values film evidence of processing over raw numbers.

examples: 2024 1.03 — Drake Maye: 6-4 3/8, 223 lbs, 32.25-inch arms. 4.60 pro day 40. Elite arm talent, dual-threat, franchise QB profile. 7,929 pass yards, 62 TDs at UNC.
2024 6.193 — Joe Milton III: 6-5, 246 lbs. Strongest arm in 2024 class. Developmental prospect with physical traits. Traded to Dallas for 2025 R5 comp pick after one year.

QB.confidence_note: Only 1 franchise QB drafted plus 1 developmental project. Maye pick was obvious at 3. Milton pick further confirms arm-talent emphasis. Profile largely inferred from scouting tree (Packers: Rodgers, Love — always valued arm talent).

EDGE.archetype_summary: Speed-to-power converter with pass-rush upside, ideally with active hands and ability to win with multiple moves. Wolf acknowledged at 2026 combine that edge rush is a deep class and 'an area of need for our team.' The one EDGE drafted (Swinson) was a twitchy, lean edge with speed-to-power and inside counters. Historical Packers/Belichick lineage suggests preference for versatile edge players who can stand up or put hand in dirt.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear minimum from limited data — Swinson measured approximately 33 inches
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: First-step quickness and explosion. Pass-rush win rate more important than raw 40 time. Vertical and broad jump as explosiveness proxies.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.65 or faster preferred. Swinson profiled as twitchy and quick off the edge.

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Speed-to-power conversion with active hands. Swinson showed 'good speed-to-power, swipe/stabs, inside counters' per team analysis.
most_valued_traits: Active hand usage and pass-rush technique
least_important_traits: Run defense consistency — Swinson fell partly due to inconsistent run defense and was still drafted
deal_breakers: One-dimensional rushers without scheme versatility
Poor effort/motor on film
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite pass-rush productivity. Swinson had 60 total pressures and 8.5 sacks in final season. PFF had him as a top-50 player, making his R5 selection a major value pick.

college_production.production_floor: Minimum 7+ sacks or 40+ pressures in final college season for Day 2 consideration
college_production.production_trajectory: Tolerant of late bloomers — Swinson didn't become a full-time starter until his final season
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — adjusts for role and scheme. Values high pass-rush win rates even in rotation.

examples: 2025 5.146 — Bradyn Swinson, LSU: ~6-3, ~245 lbs. 60 total pressures, 8.5 sacks in 2024. Speed-to-power rusher with active hands. PFF top-50 player who fell to R5. Inconsistent run defender but elite pass-rush technique.

EDGE.confidence_note: Only 1 EDGE drafted across 2 drafts (and on Day 3). EDGE was addressed in free agency (Chaisson, Landry). 2026 expected to be EDGE-heavy in draft. Profile supplemented heavily by scouting tree — Packers historically valued versatile edge players. Vrabel's Titans defense valued multiple-front versatility.

OT.archetype_summary: Athletic, technically polished pass protector with nasty competitive demeanor and proven SEC/Power 5 production. Willing to overlook short arm length if film demonstrates elite technique. Values football character, leadership, and toughness alongside athleticism. Will Campbell is the exemplar: 3-year SEC starter, sub-5.00 40 at 319 lbs, team captain, Jacobs Blocking Trophy winner — but with 32 5/8 inch arms that broke traditional thresholds.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No hard floor — Campbell drafted at 32 5/8 inches which is below traditional OT threshold of 33+. Wolf stated: 'He has a set of skills that enable him to play with that arm length.' Film trumps measurables at this position.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash relative to size (Campbell ran 4.98 at 319 lbs — fastest OL at combine). Vertical jump (Campbell: 32 inches). Explosion and footwork matter more than arm length.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Sub-5.10 preferred for tackles. Athleticism is a premium trait.

playing_style.primary_style: Technically refined pass protector first, capable run blocker second. Campbell's 'masterful kick-slides and precise hand placement' were praised.
least_important_traits: Arm length — willingness to break traditional OT arm-length thresholds when film is dominant
deal_breakers: Lack of competitive fire or toughness
Inconsistent pass protection on film regardless of measurables
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Combination of elite athleticism at size and proven SEC production. Campbell was taken 4th overall — highest OL pick for Patriots since John Hannah in 1974.

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter at Power 5 level. Campbell: 38 starts, allowed only 5 sacks over 2,553 snaps at LSU.
college_production.production_trajectory: Prefers early starters who develop over multiple seasons. Campbell was a true freshman starter.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Very high — weights SEC experience heavily. Wolf cited the value of playing in the SEC as closest thing to the NFL.

examples: 2025 1.04 — Will Campbell, OT, LSU: 6-5 7/8, 319 lbs, 32 5/8-inch arms, 4.98 40. 38 starts, 5 sacks allowed in career. Jacobs Blocking Trophy. Consensus All-American. 'Nasty' demeanor.

2024 3.68 — Caedan Wallace, OT, Penn State: Projected as R3 value. Ranked 142nd on PFF board, 186th on consensus. Solid pass protector — 72.8 PFF pass-blocking grade, 13 pressures from 359 snaps. Reach pick.
2025 7.220 — Marcus Bryant, OT, Missouri: Day 3 developmental OL with tools to compete for roster spot.

IOL.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile interior lineman with center-guard flexibility. Values range and ability to execute zone-scheme concepts — lateral agility and ability to get to second level are premium traits. Not locked into one position — wants players who can play multiple IOL spots.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Lateral agility and range in zone concepts. Wilson praised for ability to 'get out of his stance quickly' and reach blocks. Short shuttle and 3-cone matter for IOL.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Adequate athleticism to pull and reach block in zone scheme

playing_style.primary_style: Zone-scheme fit with center-guard versatility. McDaniels' offense requires IOL who can execute combo blocks and reach the second level.
most_valued_traits: Range and lateral agility in zone concepts
least_important_traits: Elite pure power/anchor — adequate is sufficient if athlete is good enough
deal_breakers: Limited positional flexibility — want players who can play multiple IOL spots
Poor communication skills
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Combination of youth, versatility, and high ceiling. Wilson drafted as a 21-year-old one-year starter.

college_production.production_floor: At least one full season as starter. Wilson was a one-year starter at Georgia. Robinson had multi-year experience at Texas A&M.
college_production.production_trajectory: Tolerant of late starters if athletic traits and football IQ are present. Wilson was 'basically the sixth man at Georgia prior to this year.'
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — values SEC/elite program context. Both IOL picks came from top programs (Georgia, Texas A&M).

examples: 2025 3.95 — Jared Wilson, C/G, Georgia: 6-3, 310 lbs. One-year starter. Wolf confirmed view as future center. Range and ability to get out of stance quickly stood out on film. Graded as high as R2 by some experts.
2024 4.103 — Layden Robinson, G, Texas A&M: Surrendered only 6 sacks across 1,206 career pass-blocking snaps. Allowed just 1 sack all of 2023. Reliable pass protector.

DT.archetype_summary: Powerful, quick-footed interior lineman with plus size and length. Values run-stuffing ability and interior anchor. Drafted Farmer as a powerful, thick-bodied interior presence to complement smaller/quicker pass rushers. Vrabel's defensive scheme requires DTs who can hold the point of attack.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: Farmer described as having 'immense length' — suggests arm length matters for interior DL
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Quick feet and first-step explosion for an interior player. Power indicators important.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a primary factor for DT

playing_style.primary_style: Run-stuffing interior lineman with enough quickness to generate occasional interior pressure. Vrabel's defense values holding the point of attack.
most_valued_traits: Size and length at the position
Quick feet for a big man
Interior pass-rush potential as a bonus
least_important_traits: Pure penetrating 3-technique pass-rush ability — not the primary archetype targeted
deal_breakers: Undersized interior players who can't hold up at the point of attack
One-dimensional sub-package-only rushers
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Combination of size, length, and quick feet

college_production.production_floor: Solid run-defense grades. Production metrics less important than physical traits for DT.
college_production.production_trajectory: No clear pattern from limited data

examples: 2025 4.137 — Joshua Farmer, DT, Florida State: ~6-3, ~300 lbs. Described as 'powerful, quick-footed interior lineman' and 'run-stuffing DT' with 'plus size and immense length.' Day 3 value pick to develop behind signed veterans.

DT.confidence_note: Only 1 DT drafted across 2 drafts. DT addressed via free agency (Tonga, Milton Williams). Packers tree historically valued DTs highly (Kenny Clark drafted R1 during Wolf's time). Confidence should increase after 2026 draft.

CB.archetype_summary: Versatile, ball-skilled corner who can play outside and in the slot. Values football IQ, movement skills, and ball production over pure speed. Willing to take late-round fliers on corners with coverage ability. Scheme under Vrabel requires corners who can play man and zone.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear threshold — limited CB data
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Movement skills and change of direction. Ball skills emphasized over raw speed.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.55 or faster preferred. Minor at 5-11, 188 lbs is a quick-twitch profile.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Versatile — able to play man and zone, outside and slot
most_valued_traits: Versatility to play multiple coverage alignments
least_important_traits: Pure straight-line speed — values movement skills over 40 time for CBs
deal_breakers: Inability to play multiple coverage schemes
Poor ball skills and instincts
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Ball-hawk ability and coverage versatility. Christian Gonzalez (pre-Wolf, but maintained as franchise corner) epitomizes what they value.

college_production.production_floor: No clear statistical floor — values film over interception totals
college_production.production_trajectory: No strong pattern from limited data
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — adjusts for quality of competition

examples: 2024 6.180 — Marcellas Dial, CB/S, South Carolina: Lone defensive pick in 2024 draft. Day 3 developmental DB.

2025 7.257 — Kobee Minor, CB, Memphis: 5-11, 182 lbs. Outside and slot versatility. Solid movement and ball skills per team evaluation. Mr. Irrelevant pick. Patriots 'had him on their radar' via a pre-draft visit.

CB.confidence_note: Only 2 CBs drafted, both on Day 3. CB largely addressed via pre-Wolf acquisitions (Gonzalez in 2023) and free agency (Carlton Davis III). Profile heavily inferred from scheme requirements and scouting tree.

S.archetype_summary: Versatile, high-IQ safety who can play multiple roles — box, deep half, man coverage. Communication skills and football intelligence are paramount. Wolf stated safeties 'have to be able to play down in the box, man cover, tackle in space' and values interchangeability. Craig Woodson exemplifies this: a smart communicator from Cal with versatility.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Athleticism and instincts more valued than any single combine test. Change of direction matters for coverage.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Adequate speed to cover — not a pure speed position in their evaluation

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile, interchangeable safety who can play multiple roles in the defensive backfield
most_valued_traits: Communication skills — Cowden specifically cited this as what 'sets him apart' for Woodson
Versatility to play box, deep, and man coverage
least_important_traits: Pure straight-line speed — not prioritized over instincts and IQ
deal_breakers: Poor communicator or low football IQ
One-dimensional safety who can only play one role
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite communication skills and defensive quarterback ability. Woodson was taken in R4 despite being seen as a possible reach by consensus — Cowden defended the pick citing process and evaluation.

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter at a program known for producing smart defensive players. Cowden noted Cal 'had a history of having really good communicators.'
college_production.production_trajectory: Values multi-year starters over one-year wonders
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — adjusts for program pedigree and defensive system

examples: 2025 4.106 — Craig Woodson, S, California: ~6-0, ~205 lbs. 'Great person, high level of character, really smart football player.' Communication skills were primary selling point. Perceived as a reach by consensus but defended by front office.
2024 6.180 — Marcellas Dial, CB/S, South Carolina: Listed as CB but cross-trained at safety. Developmental Day 3 DB.

LB.archetype_summary: No off-ball linebackers have been drafted in early rounds under Wolf's tenure. LB has been addressed primarily through free agency (Spillane, Gibbens). Profile must be inferred from scheme and scouting tree. Vrabel's defense historically values sideline-to-sideline speed, coverage ability, and tackling in space. Packers tree values athletic LBs who can play in space.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed and lateral agility for coverage assignments. 40 time matters for LBs in this scheme. 3-cone and short shuttle as indicators of change of direction.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.60 or faster preferred for starting-caliber LB

playing_style.primary_style: Coverage-capable, sideline-to-sideline LB who can tackle in space. Vrabel's defense requires LBs to match backs and TEs in coverage.
most_valued_traits: Coverage ability against backs and TEs
least_important_traits: Pure thumper/run-stuffer ability without coverage skills
deal_breakers: Inability to play in coverage
Limited range and lateral movement
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite coverage ability for the position

college_production.production_floor: No data — no LBs drafted in early rounds

LB.confidence_note: Zero off-ball LBs drafted in rounds 1–4 across 2 drafts. LB completely addressed via free agency. Profile inferred from Vrabel's defensive scheme and FA signings (Spillane — coverage-capable, high-IQ LB). Packers tree traditionally values athletic LBs.

WR.archetype_summary: Size-speed-hands hybrid who can win contested catches and play multiple alignment spots. Not drafting pure burners — values reliable hands, body control, contested catch ability, and physical route running over pure deep speed. Polk and Baker were both 6-1+, 200+ lbs with strong hands and below-average long speed. Kyle Williams added more of a route-running/YAC dimension. Overall: wants WRs who are 'quarterback-friendly' and reliable, not just athletes.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No clear threshold — Baker and Polk both around 32+ inches
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Broad jump and vertical as explosiveness indicators. Burst score more valued than 40 time. Hands/catch radius not measured at combine but evaluated heavily on film.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Not a hard cutoff. Polk ran 4.52, Baker ran 4.54, Williams is a quicker/YAC type. Not targeting burners — all 3 WR picks ran 4.50+ at combine.

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile, alignment-flexible receiver who can play X, Z, or slot. Strong hands and contested-catch ability are the common thread.
most_valued_traits: Willingness to block in the run game
least_important_traits: Pure deep speed — has consistently drafted WRs who run 4.50+ rather than sub-4.40 burners
deal_breakers: Unreliable hands and high drop rates
Inability to win contested situations despite adequate size
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite contested catch ability and reliable hands. Polk's 'knack for making tough plays look routine' was specifically cited.

college_production.production_floor: 50+ catches in final college season for Day 2 consideration. Polk: 69 catches, 1,159 yards, 9 TDs in 2023. Baker: 52 catches, 1,139 yards, 7 TDs (21.9 YPC). Williams: 65 catches, 1,077 yards. All were productive starters.
college_production.production_trajectory: Tolerant of transfers and late bloomers if final-year production is strong. Baker transferred from Alabama to UCF. Polk transferred from Texas Tech to Washington. Williams transferred from UNLV to Wazzu.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — adjusts for system and QB quality. Noted Baker's productivity despite 'three different quarterbacks.'

examples: 2024 2.37 — Ja'Lynn Polk, WR, Washington: 6-1, 203 lbs, 4.52 40. 69 catches, 1,159 yards, 9 TDs. Contested-catch specialist with strong hands. QB-friendly target. Run-blocker. Struggled in NFL, traded after 1 season.
2024 4.110 — Javon Baker, WR, UCF: 6-1, 202 lbs, 4.54 40, 32.25-inch arms. Led Big 12 with 1,139 yards. 21.9 YPC. Vertical threat with ball-tracking ability. Developmental route runner.
2025 3.69 — Kyle Williams, WR, Washington State: Route runner with YAC juice. 14 catches on 20+ yard passes in 2024. Explosive change of direction. Wolf noted his 'really productive junior year.'

TE.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile tight end with receiving upside and YAC ability. Day 3 investment only so far. Jaheim Bell was a late-round athletic freak with position flexibility. Values ball skills and ability with the ball in his hands over traditional inline blocking.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Vertical jump and broad jump as receiving-TE athleticism indicators. YAC ability important.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.65 or faster preferred for receiving TE

playing_style.primary_style: Receiving-first tight end with athletic ability and YAC upside. Not a traditional inline blocker.
most_valued_traits: Versatility to line up inline and flexed out
least_important_traits: Traditional inline blocking ability — not a priority for early TE picks
deal_breakers: Pure blocking TEs without receiving dimension
Stiff athletes without YAC ability
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athletic profile for the position

college_production.production_floor: No clear floor from limited data. Bell: 73.4 PFF grade in 2023, 7th among FBS TEs in YAC.

examples: 2024 7.231 — Jaheim Bell, TE, Florida State: Athletic TE with exceptional YAC ability (ranked 7th among FBS TEs with 315 YAC in 2023). 73.4 PFF grade. Multi-positional athlete. Great value in R7. Also signed CJ Dippre as UDFA in 2025.

TE.confidence_note: Only 1 TE drafted (R7). TE addressed via FA signings (Hooper, Henry retained). McDaniels' offense historically features TEs prominently — expect more TE investment as roster stabilizes.

RB.archetype_summary: Dynamic, explosive playmaker with elite speed, big-play ability, and pass-catching/pass-protection versatility. TreVeyon Henderson represents the archetype perfectly: blazing 4.43 speed, 38.5-inch vertical, home-run threat, capable in pass protection, and reliable ball security. Willing to invest Day 2 pick on the right RB profile despite position being traditionally devalued.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: Not a factor for RB evaluation
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed (40 time), explosion (vertical jump, broad jump). Henderson: 4.43 40, 38.5-inch vertical, 128-inch broad jump — 92nd percentile athleticism.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster for Day 2 RB. Henderson's 4.43 was elite.

playing_style.primary_style: Explosive, speed-based runner with home-run ability and three-down capability. Not a pure power back.
most_valued_traits: Pass-catching skills and route running from backfield
Ball security — Henderson had zero fumbles at Ohio State
least_important_traits: Pure between-the-tackles power — values speed and explosion over grind-it-out running
deal_breakers: Fumble-prone runners
One-dimensional backs who can't contribute in pass game or protect the QB
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite speed combined with pass-protection ability and ball security. Henderson's complete skill set justified a R2 investment.

college_production.production_floor: 3,000+ career rushing yards or 1,000+ yards in final season. Henderson: 3,761 career rushing yards, 48 TDs at Ohio State.
college_production.production_trajectory: Tolerant of shared backfields if talent is obvious. Henderson split carries throughout Ohio State career but showed elite production per touch.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — adjusted for Henderson's role split at Ohio State. 7.0+ YPC with 1,000+ rushing yards demonstrated elite efficiency.

examples: 2025 2.38 — TreVeyon Henderson, RB, Ohio State: 5-10, 202 lbs, 4.43 40, 38.5-inch vertical, 128-inch broad. National champion. 3,761 career rushing yards, 48 TDs, zero fumbles. 911 rushing yards, 9 TDs as rookie plus 35 catches. Multiple 50+ yard TDs. AFC Offensive Player of the Month (November).

RB.confidence_note: Only 1 RB drafted but it was a R2 investment — significant commitment to the position. Henderson's profile is very clear: explosive, dynamic, three-down back. Packers tree influence visible (Aaron Jones, a similar speed/explosion back, was a Wolf-era Packers find in R5).

reach_tolerance.notes: In R1, has taken consensus top-5 talent both years (Maye at 3, Campbell at 4) — no reaching. Reached significantly in R3 2024: Caedan Wallace ranked 142nd on PFF board, 186th on consensus, taken at pick 68 — approximately a 75-pick reach. Craig Woodson in R4 2025 was considered the 'biggest reach' of the class by outside consensus. Willing to reach on Day 2-3 for players they've evaluated highly in their own process. Cowden defended reaches: 'We have a process here that we believe in.'

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Josh McDaniels West Coast / Erhardt-Perkins system with zone-run concepts. Dynasty-era system returned. Thomas Brown adds Andy Reid tree passing concepts. Values versatile OL who can execute zone concepts. Pass-first when leading, physical run game to close out. Quick passing game with play-action to set up deep shots.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Multiple-front defense under Terrell Williams (DC) / Zak Kuhr (promoted DC for 2026). Vrabel-influenced physical, defensive-minded identity. 3-4 base with ability to flex to multiple fronts. Values versatile defensive players who can play multiple roles. Secondary must be interchangeable. Wolf noted defense needs to 'affect the quarterback from interior or exterior.'
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: Vrabel installed new defensive system in 2025, replacing Jerod Mayo era. Zak Kuhr promoted to DC for 2026 after Williams battled cancer. McDaniels returned as OC — continuity from dynasty era. Scheme is stabilizing but still evolving. Defensive coordinator change from Williams to Kuhr could slightly adjust personnel needs.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderately conservative. R1 picks have been high-floor, safe selections (Maye was consensus top-3 QB, Campbell was consensus top-5 OT). Day 2-3 has shown more willingness to take calculated risks on developmental players (Milton at QB, Bell at TE, Swinson falling due to interview concerns). Overall: safe in R1, calculated gambles on Day 2-3.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate-to-high recency bias. After historically bad offensive season in 2023-2024, drafted 7 of 8 offensive players in 2024. After making Super Bowl in 2025, identified pass rush and OL depth as needs based on playoff exposure. Wolf acknowledged Super Bowl performance changed perception of needs.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Willing to move on quickly. Traded Joe Milton III to Dallas for a R5 pick after one year. Ja'Lynn Polk, a R2 pick, was reportedly on the roster bubble and traded to New Orleans after disappointing first year. Does not double down on failed picks — reinvests at the position (drafted Kyle Williams to replace Polk's production).
psychological_profile.combine_description: Balanced between film and measurables, but film is the tiebreaker. Drafted Campbell despite arm-length concerns because film was dominant. Wolf stated: 'I know everyone talks about the arm length, but he has a set of skills that enable him to play with that arm length.' However, athletic testing matters — Henderson's 4.43 and Campbell's 4.98 at 319 lbs clearly boosted their profiles. Ron Wolf's system is a 'simple numbers-only grading scale' that Eliot still follows.
psychological_profile.character_description: Very high character emphasis. Cowden stated 'if you don't get the person right, then a lot of that other stuff can go by the wayside.' Vrabel emphasized 'loyal, trustworthy coaches who are diverse in background.' Campbell praised for being a 'great person' and team captain. Woodson praised for character. Henderson was a straight-A student with 4.0 GPA. Every press conference mentions character and culture fit. Swinson reportedly fell partly due to pre-draft interview concerns — Patriots still took him but only in R5.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Franchise QB is the cornerstone. Went all-in on Maye at 3 — no hedging. Also willing to draft developmental QB projects (Milton in R6). Now that franchise QB is in place, will not draft another QB high. Building everything around protecting and weaponizing Maye.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Leans productive college players. Both R1 picks were elite multi-year producers (Maye — 7,929 pass yards; Campbell — 38 starts, Jacobs Blocking Trophy). WR picks all had 1,000+ yard seasons. Henderson had 3,761 career rushing yards. However, willing to bet on projection in late rounds (Milton's arm, Bell's athleticism, Swinson's pass-rush potential). In tiebreakers, production wins.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: All WRs drafted in rounds 2-3 had 50+ catches and 1,000+ yards in final college season
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: 3,000+ career yards or 1,000+ final season. Henderson: 3,761 career, 1,016 final year
production_thresholds_by_position.OT: Multi-year starter at Power 5 level with elite PFF grades. Campbell: 38 starts, 95th percentile pass-blocking grade on true pass sets
production_thresholds_by_position.QB: Multi-year starter with 3,500+ passing yards in breakout season
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: No clear production floor — Swinson had 8.5 sacks and 60 pressures but only 1 year as full-time starter
production_thresholds_by_position.IOL: At least 1 full season as starter at Power 5 level

college_production_preferences.notes: Packers lineage shows strong production emphasis. Wolf personally found productive Packers players like Aaron Jones and Greg Jennings. Transfer portal players are acceptable if production post-transfer is strong. Not breakout-age sensitive — multiple picks were transfers who broke out late (Polk, Baker, Williams).

QB.notes: Arm talent is the primary measurable. 6-2+ height and 220+ lbs preferred. Mobility valued but not measured by 40 time.

OT.notes: Film over measurables. Campbell drafted at 32 5/8 inch arms, well below traditional 33+ threshold. Sub-5.00 40 at 315+ lbs is elite and valued.

WR.40_yard_range: 4.50-4.55 seems acceptable. No sub-4.45 WR drafted. Burst and explosion tests (vertical, broad) more valued than 40.

RB.notes: Henderson at 4.43 and 38.5 vertical set a clear athletic bar. Speed is premium for RB evaluation.

S.notes: Football IQ and communication more valued than any athletic test for safeties.

CB.notes: Only Day 3 CBs drafted. Movement skills valued over straight-line speed.

EDGE.notes: Pass-rush win rate and technique matter more than size. Swinson was lean and twitchy.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Mostly soft preferences rather than hard cutoffs. Film-first evaluation philosophy. The Campbell arm-length example proves willingness to break traditional measurable thresholds when film is dominant.

size_preferences_by_position.WR: 6-0+ and 200+ lbs preferred — all 3 WR picks were 6-1+, 200+ lbs
size_preferences_by_position.QB: 6-2+ and 220+ preferred. Both QB picks (Maye 6-4/223, Milton 6-5/246) were big-framed

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: Vertical jump and broad jump as explosiveness indicators across positions. 40-yard dash relative to position weight is important (Campbell's sub-5.00 at 319 was cited widely). Short shuttle and 3-cone for IOL and EDGE evaluation. Raw 40 time matters most for RB and WR. Arm velocity for QBs — not formally tested but evaluated informally.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film-first, measurables as confirmatory data. Wolf/Cowden process trusts film evaluation over testing. Campbell example is definitive: taken top-5 despite arms that would have been a red flag to many teams. Wolf's stated approach: 'The tape, there's not one play on there where you say that's because he has shorter arms.' Ron Wolf's scouting system is a 'simple numbers-only grading scale' based on film evaluation.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Address biggest team need with safest, most polished prospect available. High-floor, multi-year producers from elite programs. Maye (can't-miss QB) and Campbell (3-year SEC starter, All-American) both fit this pattern. Will not swing for upside in R1.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: Mix of BPA and need-filling. Willing to reach for targeted players. Trades down to accumulate extra picks in this range. Henderson (R2, RB) was need + value. Williams (R3, WR) and Wilson (R3, C) were need-fills. 2024 R2 was WR Polk — clear need after worst-offense season. Shift from pure BPA to targeted need-filling happens in R2.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, special teams contributors, and calculated gambles. Drafted a kicker (Borregales, R6), long snapper (Ashby, R7), and Mr. Irrelevant CB (Minor, R7) in 2025. Day 3 is where character risks and physical tools get prioritized — Swinson fell due to interview concerns but was taken as a value pick. Milton (raw QB with elite arm, R6) is a classic Day 3 traits-over-production bet. Also uses Day 3 for OL depth (Bryant, R7).
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: Treats comp picks similarly to regular picks but may use them for higher-risk players. Farmer (comp R4 via SEA) was a developmental DT; Ashby and Minor (comp R7s via KC) were specialists and developmental players.

QB.note: Maye became borderline MVP candidate in year 2 — elite hit. Milton traded after 1 year.

OT.note: Campbell started at LT as rookie but struggled in playoffs. Wallace hasn't emerged. Bryant is developmental. Too early to judge.

WR.note: Polk was a bust (traded after 1 year). Baker struggled. Williams showed promise as a rookie. WR evaluation is a clear weak spot.

IOL.note: Wilson started at LG as rookie. Robinson has contributed. Both early but solid.

RB.note: Henderson was AFC Offensive Player of the Month as a rookie. Excellent hit.

S.note: Woodson and Dial are developmental. Too early to judge.

EDGE.note: Swinson was cut from initial 53 — spent most of year on practice squad. Very early.

CB.note: Minor was Mr. Irrelevant. Low investment.

blind_spots: WR evaluation has been inconsistent — Polk was a bust, Baker struggled. Pattern of drafting physical, non-explosive WRs who don't separate at NFL level. Simulation should slightly discount WR pick confidence.
strengths: QB evaluation appears strong (Maye). RB evaluation (Henderson). Offensive line investment has been heavy and shows commitment to the trenches. Day 3 value-finding appears strong (Swinson, Henderson's steal in R2).

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Moderate-to-aggressive spender who uses FA to fill immediate starter-level needs, then drafts for long-term building. 2025 FA: signed Diggs, Hollins (WR), Bradbury (C), Moses (OT), Tonga and Milton Williams (DT), Chaisson and Landry (EDGE), Spillane (LB), Davis III (CB) — filled many holes to set up draft for long-term pieces.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: After signing veteran starters at a position, still drafts at that position for development. Signed Bradbury at C, then drafted Wilson (C) in R3. Signed Diggs and Hollins at WR, then drafted Williams (WR) in R3. Uses FA to bridge gaps while drafting young successors.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at premium positions (QB, OT, CB, EDGE), use FA aggressively to fill the rest while young players develop. 2025 was a master class in this approach: FA addressed defense wholesale, draft focused on offense and long-term building. Wolf stated: 'Every year is an opportunity in free agency and the draft to improve the roster... It's trades, it's waiver claims, it's restricted free agency, all those things.'

notable_patterns: 7 of 8 picks in 2024 were offensive players — heavily offensive drafting when team needs demand it
Both R1 picks addressed single biggest team need (QB in 2024, OT in 2025) — need drives R1 despite BPA rhetoric
Consistently trades down to accumulate extra picks (Belichick influence). Traded down in R2 2024 and twice in R3 2025
Heavy investment in offensive line: 5 OL across 2 drafts (Campbell, Wallace, Robinson, Wilson, Bryant)
Drafted 3 WRs in first 4 rounds across 2 drafts — offensive weapons prioritized for Maye
Character and culture fit repeatedly emphasized in every press conference and pick explanation
Willing to break traditional measurable thresholds (Campbell arm length) when film justifies it
Day 3 used for special teams (K, LS) and developmental athletes — Belichick-era pattern continued

biggest_reaches.context: OT was a massive need. Reached significantly for positional need after missing on preferred targets.
Perceived as biggest reach of 2025 class by consensus. Cowden defended: 'We have a process here that we believe in.'

biggest_steals.context: PFF top-50 player who fell to R5. Pre-draft interview concerns contributed to slide. 60 pressures and 8.5 sacks at LSU.
Graded as high as R2 by some experts. Patriots traded down twice to this spot and still landed him.

confidence_notes: 2 full drafts as primary decision-maker provide moderate data. Scouting tree (Packers/Ron Wolf) is exceptionally well-documented and clearly visible in Wolf's approach. Vrabel's influence adds a defensive/toughness dimension that is newer and still being calibrated. Wolf ranked 2nd in 2025 NFL Executive of the Year voting. The triumvirate of Wolf, Vrabel, and Cowden appears stable heading into 2026. Profile should be refreshed after 2026 draft, which will be the first where the team picks late (31st) rather than in the top 5.
